Title: Distributional Learning of Graph Languages Generated by Fixed-Interface Clause Systems

URL Source: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26333v1

Published Time: Thu, 30 Apr 2026 00:29:24 GMT

Number of Pages: 39

Markdown Content:
# Distributional Learning of Graph Languages Generated by Fixed-Interface Clause Systems 

Takayoshi Shoudai Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Fukuoka Institute of Technology Fukuoka 811-0295, Japan 

shodai@fit.ac.jp 

Satoshi Matsumoto Department of Mathematical Sciences Tokai University Hiratsuka 259-1292, Japan 

matsumoto@tokai.ac.jp 

Yusuke Suzuki and Tomoyuki Uchida Graduate School of Information Sciences Hiroshima City University Hiroshima 731-3194, Japan 

{y-suzuki, uchida }@hiroshima-cu.ac.jp 

Abstract 

Distributional learning provides a useful framework for studying the learnability of structured languages from positive data. In this pa-per, we extend this framework to graph languages generated by fixed-interface clause systems. We give an explicit formulation of fixed-interface graph pattern clause systems and define the associated learning model using posi-tive presentations and membership queries. Within this setting, we consider a bounded class of graph languages satisfying the finite con-text property under a bounded-degree assumption. The bounds are expressed by a parameter tuple (∆ , m, s, t, w, d ), which controls both the generated graph class and the structural complexity of the clause systems. The learning algorithm constructs hypotheses from ordered bound-ary representations induced by the observed positive examples. These 

1

> arXiv:2604.26333v1 [cs.FL] 29 Apr 2026

representations make explicit the interface information needed to com-pare contexts and to test candidate clauses by membership queries. We prove the correctness of the algorithm step by step: target con-texts eventually appear in the observed sample, target clauses are re-constructed over the corresponding predicate representatives, and spu-rious non-fact clauses are eventually excluded by membership queries. As a consequence, for every fixed parameter tuple (∆ , m, s, t, w, d ), the target language is identifiable in the limit from positive data and membership queries. We also analyze the complexity of the hypothesis update process. Using the bounded-degree assumption and the fixed interface-rank bound, we show that only polynomially many ordered boundary rep-resentations, predicate symbols, clause candidates, and membership queries are needed at each stage. Hence the learner has polynomial-time update on FICSL FCP ∆ (m, s, t, w, d ). Thus, the paper provides a parameterized reformulation of distri-butional learning for regular FGS-style graph languages in a fixed-interface setting, making the roles of boundary information, clause structure, and structural parameters explicit. 

Keywords: distributional learning; graph languages; fixed-interface clause systems; finite context property; membership queries; polynomial-time up-date 

# 1 Introduction 

Distributional learning has been one of the central approaches in algorith-mic learning theory for establishing learnability from positive data. It was initiated by Clark and Eyraud [1] for subclasses of context-free grammars and was subsequently extended to broader grammar classes and to more general structured objects [4, 11, 12]. More recently, this line of research has continued to develop on the string side, where richer non-terminal rep-resentations have been used to extend distributional learning from positive data and membership queries to larger classes of context-free languages [3]. A common theme throughout these studies is that learnability becomes ac-cessible once the target objects admit an appropriate decomposition into contexts and substructures. The present paper is concerned with graph languages. As graph-based counterparts of grammatical formalisms for strings and trees, graph gram-mars have been studied in several variants. Among them, formal graph sys-tems (FGSs), introduced by Uchida et al. [10], provide a logic-programming-based formalism in which graph patterns serve as structured objects. Reg-2ular and restricted variants of FGSs have been used as a basis for studying learnability properties of graph languages [2, 7, 8]. From the viewpoint of expressiveness, regular FGSs are closely related to hyperedge replacement grammars; from the viewpoint of learning, they provide a natural graph-structured setting in which distributional methods can be investigated. Several learning-theoretic results for graph-based formalisms are already known. Okada et al. [6] established exact learning results for certain graph pattern classes in the minimally adequate teacher model. Hara and Shoudai [2] studied polynomial-time MAT learning of c-deterministic reg-ular formal graph systems. Shoudai et al. [7] investigated regular FGS languages of bounded degree under positive data and membership queries in a distributional-learning setting. More recently, Shoudai et al. [8] studied polynomial-time PAC learnability for bounded classes of variable-hereditary FGS languages under bounded degree and bounded treewidth assumptions. These results indicate that graph languages admit a substantial learning theory, but they also suggest that the roles of boundary information, clause structure, and structural parameters deserve a more explicit treatment than in the earlier formulations. The aim of this paper is to formulate and analyze a distributional-learning framework for graph languages generated by fixed-interface clause systems. The point is not merely to restate an earlier result in different notation. Rather, by rewriting the underlying regular-FGS-style mecha-nism in a fixed-interface language, we make explicit the boundary structure that is already implicit in fragment-based decompositions. Although the target language consists of plain graphs, the learning procedure works with graphs with interfaces obtained by cutting observed graphs along designated boundary vertices. This interface structure is essential both for the defini-tion of context candidates and for the parameterized complexity analysis of hypothesis updates. Within this framework, we consider a bounded class of graph languages satisfying the finite context property under a bounded-degree assumption. The bounds are given explicitly by a parameter tuple (∆ , m, s, t, w, d ), which controls, among other things, the generated graph class, the number of clauses, the number of variable hyperedges, the number of body atoms, and the interface ranks. This parameterized formulation makes it possible to isolate which structural restrictions are responsible for learnability and which are responsible for polynomial-time update. In particular, compared with the ILP 2016 treatment, the present formulation replaces the role of a fixed Chomsky-normal-form-style bound on rule shapes by explicit param-eter bounds that can be traced throughout the correctness and complexity 3arguments. For this class, we present an oracle-guided learning algorithm based on ordered boundary representations induced by the observed positive exam-ples. The algorithm constructs hypotheses by introducing predicate repre-sentatives from these boundary representations and by admitting bounded fixed-interface clause candidates through membership-query tests. We prove, step by step, that target contexts eventually occur in the observed sample, that target clauses are reconstructed over the corresponding predicate rep-resentatives, and that spurious non-fact clauses are eventually excluded by membership queries. We also analyze the update complexity and prove that the learner has polynomial-time update. This paper serves as a full journal version of the ILP 2016 conference paper [7], in which all proofs were omitted. Beyond providing complete proofs, the present paper makes three further contributions. First, we re-formulate the framework using fixed-interface clause systems and ordered boundary representations, making the boundary structure explicit rather than leaving it implicit in fragment-based decompositions. Second, we make explicit that the bounded treewidth assumption used in [7] is not needed for the present distributional-learning argument: the result holds under the bounded-degree assumption together with the fixed structural param-eters. Bounded treewidth was a natural restriction in our earlier work [9] on graph-pattern matching, where tree decompositions were used to ob-tain polynomial-time algorithms for partial k-tree patterns. In contrast, the present distributional-learning framework does not use tree decomposi-tions in the construction of context candidates or in the membership-query filtering argument. This observation clarifies why the bounded treewidth assumption imposed in the ILP 2016 formulation is not essential for the present learning argument. Third, we introduce an explicit parameter tuple (∆ , m, s, t, w, d ) that allows the structural restrictions responsible for learn-ability and polynomial-time update to be traced throughout the correctness and complexity arguments. At the same time, since the construction is based on context decomposition and membership-query filtering, the paper can also be read as a graph-structured generalization of the corresponding string-based distributional-learning framework [3]. The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces graphs with interfaces, graph patterns, and fixed-interface clause systems. Section 3 defines the learning model. Section 4 presents the learning algo-rithm, proves its correctness, and establishes the parameterized polynomial-time update bound. 42 Preliminaries 

2.1 Graph with interface and ordered boundary representa-tion 

We first recall the notion of a graph with interface and introduce ordered boundary representations. Interfaces specify the vertices through which a graph fragment can be composed with another fragment. Ordered boundary representations will later be used to enumerate, from positive examples, the finitely many context candidates needed by the learning algorithm. 

Definition 1 (Graph with interface) . Let Σ V and Σ E be finite alphabets of vertex labels and edge labels, respectively. A graph with interface is a tuple 

P = ( V, E, ϕ, ψ, ι ), where (1) V is a finite set of vertices, (2) E ⊆ {u, v } | u, v ∈ V, u ̸ = v is a finite set of edges, (3) ϕ : V → ΣV is a vertex-labeling function, (4) ψ : E → ΣE is an edge-labeling function, and (5) ι = ( v1, . . . , v r) is an ordered list of distinct vertices in V .The vertices appearing in ι are called the interface vertices of P , and the integer r is called the interface rank of P . If r = 0, then P is called closed .

Remark 1. Every graph is identified with the graph with empty interface obtained by regarding it as ( V, E, ϕ, ψ, ()) .

Definition 2 (Subgraph with interface) . Let G = ( V, E, ϕ, ψ ) be a finite undirected labeled graph, where E ⊆ {u, v } | u, v ∈ V, u ̸ = v . A subgraph with interface of G is a graph with interface K = ( VK , E K , ϕ K , ψ K , ι K )such that VK ⊆ V, EK ⊆ E, ϕ K = ϕ|VK , ψK = ψ|EK , and ιK =(b1, . . . , b r) is an ordered list of distinct vertices of VK . The vertices b1, . . . , b r

are called the boundary vertices of K, and r is called the boundary rank of 

K.

Definition 3 (Ordered boundary representation) . Let G = ( V, E, ϕ, ψ ) be a finite undirected labeled graph, and let K = ( VK , E K , ϕ K , ψ K , ι K ) be a sub-graph with interface of G, where ιK = ( b1, . . . , b r). Put BK = {b1, . . . , b r}.

The boundary edge set of K is EB (K) = {e ∈ EK | e∩BK̸ = ∅} . The ordered boundary representation of K is the pair ( β, E B (K)) , where β = ( b1, . . . , b r).

5The order of the vertices in β is part of the representation. Thus, the same boundary vertex set with different orders gives different ordered boundary representations. 

Definition 4 (Valid ordered boundary specification) . Let G = ( V, E, ϕ, ψ )be a finite undirected labeled graph. An ordered boundary specification in G

is a pair ( β, E B ), where β = ( b1, . . . , b r) is an ordered list of distinct vertices of G, and EB ⊆ E.Let B = {b1, . . . , b r}. We say that ( β, E B ) is valid in G if every edge in EB

is incident with at least one vertex in B, that is, e ∩ B̸ = ∅ for every e ∈

EB .

For a valid ordered boundary specification ( β, E B ), define SG(β, E B ) = 

{v ∈ V \ B | { b, v } ∈ EB for some b ∈ B}. Let IG(β, E B ) be the set of all vertices of G−B that are reachable in G−B from some vertex in SG(β, E B ). Here G − B denotes the graph obtained from G by deleting all vertices in 

B and all edges incident with them. The subgraph with interface determined by ( β, E B ) is KG(β, E B ) = (VK , E K , ϕ K , ψ K , β ), where VK = B ∪ IG(β, E B ) and EK = EB ∪ {x, y } ∈ 

E | x, y ∈ IG(β, E B ) . The label functions are the restrictions ϕK =

ϕ|VK , ψK = ψ|EK .

Remark 2. An arbitrary pair ( β, E B ) need not be a valid ordered boundary specification. In the construction below, such pairs are enumerated as can-didates, and only valid ones are used to construct subgraphs with interface. 

Lemma 1. Let G = ( V, E, ϕ, ψ ) be a finite undirected labeled graph. Ev-ery valid ordered boundary specification (β, E B ) in G determines a unique subgraph with interface KG(β, E B ).Proof. Let β = ( b1, . . . , b r) and put B = {b1, . . . , b r}. Since ( β, E B ) is valid, every edge in EB is an edge of G incident with at least one vertex in B.The set SG(β, E B ) is determined uniquely by G, B, and EB . The graph 

G − B is also uniquely determined by G and B. Hence the set IG(β, E B ) of vertices reachable in G − B from SG(β, E B ) is uniquely determined. Therefore the vertex set VK = B ∪ IG(β, E B ) is uniquely determined. The edge set EK = EB ∪ {x, y } ∈ E | x, y ∈ IG(β, E B ) is also uniquely determined. The label functions ϕK = ϕ|VK , ψK = ψ|EK are uniquely determined by the label functions of G, and the interface is exactly the ordered tuple β.Thus KG(β, E B ) = ( VK , E K , ϕ K , ψ K , β ) is uniquely determined. 6Lemma 2. Let G = ( V, E, ϕ, ψ ) be a finite undirected labeled graph. Given an ordered tuple β = ( b1, . . . , b r) of vertices and a set EB , it can be decided in time O(|V |+|E|) whether (β, E B ) is a valid ordered boundary specification in G. If it is valid, the corresponding subgraph with interface KG(β, E B ) can be constructed in time O(|V | + |E|).

Proof. First, we check whether b1, . . . , b r are vertices of G and are pairwise distinct. This can be done in O(r) time using marking on the vertex set. Next, let B = {b1, . . . , b r}. For every edge e ∈ EB , we check whether 

e ∈ E and whether e ∩ B̸ = ∅. Using a standard adjacency representation, this takes O(|EB |) time after the graph has been stored in a form that supports edge membership queries. Since EB ⊆ E, we have |EB | ≤ | E|.Thus this validity check is bounded by O(|V | + |E|). If the check fails, then ( β, E B ) is not valid. Suppose that the check succeeds. We compute SG(β, E B ) = {v ∈ V \ B | { b, v } ∈ EB for some b ∈ B}.

This is done by scanning the edges in EB , and therefore takes O(|EB |) time. We then perform a breadth-first search or depth-first search in the graph 

G − B, starting from all vertices in SG(β, E B ). This computes IG(β, E B ) in time O(|V | + |E|). Finally, we construct VK = B ∪ IG(β, E B ) and EK = EB ∪ {x, y } ∈ 

E | x, y ∈ IG(β, E B ) by scanning the edge set E once. This also takes 

O(|V | + |E|) time. The label functions are obtained by restricting ϕ and ψ,which adds only linear time. Therefore validity can be decided, and the corresponding subgraph with interface can be constructed when valid, in time O(|V | + |E|). 

Lemma 3. Let G = ( V, E, ϕ, ψ ) be a finite undirected labeled graph of max-imum degree at most ∆, and put n = |V |. For every fixed r ≥ 0, the number of ordered boundary specifications (β, E B ) of boundary rank r is at most 

nr2r∆.

Proof. First, we count the number of possible ordered boundary tuples β =(b1, . . . , b r) of distinct vertices of G. This number is n(n − 1) · · · (n − r + 1) ≤

nr.

Fix such a tuple β, and let B = {b1, . . . , b r}. Since G has maximum degree at most ∆, the number of edges of G incident with at least one vertex of B is at most r∆. The boundary edge set EB is chosen as a subset of these incident edges. Therefore the number of possible choices of EB is at most 2 r∆.

7Multiplying the number of choices of β and EB , we obtain the upper bound nr2r∆. This proves the lemma. 

Lemma 4. Let G = ( V, E, ϕ, ψ ) be a finite undirected labeled graph of max-imum degree at most ∆, and put n = |V |. For a fixed boundary-rank bound 

w, all valid ordered boundary specifications of rank at most w, together with their corresponding subgraphs with interface, can be enumerated in time 

O␀∆ 2 w∆nw+1 . In particular, if w and ∆ are fixed constants, this time is polynomial in n.Proof. Fix r ≤ w. By Lemma 3, the number of candidate ordered boundary specifications of rank r is at most nr2r∆.

For each candidate ( β, E B ), Lemma 2 shows that validity can be decided and, if valid, the corresponding subgraph with interface can be constructed in time O(|V | + |E|). Since G has maximum degree at most ∆, we have 

|E| = O(∆ n). Hence the time per candidate is O(∆ n).

Therefore, for rank r, the total time is bounded by O␀nr2r∆ · ∆n. Sum-ming over r = 0 , 1, . . . , w , we obtain Pwr=0 O␀nr2r∆ ·∆n = O␀∆ 2 w∆nw+1 ,

because w is fixed and the largest term occurs at r = w up to a constant factor depending only on w and ∆. Thus all valid ordered boundary specifications of rank at most w, and their corresponding subgraphs with interface, can be enumerated within the claimed time bound. 

Corollary 1. Let Dn = {G1, . . . , G n} be a finite set of positive examples, and let Sn = Pni=1 |V (Gi)|. Assume that every Gi has maximum degree at most ∆. For fixed w and ∆, all valid ordered boundary specifications of rank at most w arising from the graphs in Dn, together with their correspond-ing subgraphs with interface, can be enumerated in time polynomial in Sn.More precisely, the total time is bounded by O ␀∆ 2 w∆ Pni=1 |V (Gi)|w+1  ≤

O ␀∆ 2 w∆Sw+1 

> n

 .

Proof. Apply Lemma 4 to each graph Gi. If ni = |V (Gi)|, then the time required for Gi is O␀∆ 2 w∆nw+1 

> i

. Summing over i = 1 , . . . , n , the total time is O ␀∆ 2 w∆ Pni=1 nw+1 

> i

 . Since Pni=1 nw+1  

> i

≤ (Pni=1 ni)w+1 = Sw+1  

> n

,

we obtain O ␀∆ 2 w∆Sw+1 

> n

 . For fixed w and ∆, this is polynomial in Sn.

2.2 Graph patterns with variable hyperedges 

Definition 5 (Graph pattern) . Let Σ V and Σ E be finite alphabets of vertex labels and edge labels, respectively, and let X be an infinite set of variable 8labels equipped with a rank function rank : X → N. A graph pattern with variable hyperedges is a tuple G = ( V, E, H, ϕ, ψ, λ, ports , ι ), where (1) V is a finite set of vertices, (2) E ⊆ {u, v } | u, v ∈ V, u ̸ = v is a finite set of ordinary edges, (3) H is a finite set of hyperedges, (4) ϕ : V → ΣV is a vertex-labeling function, (5) ψ : E → ΣE is an edge-labeling function, (6) λ : H → X is a variable-labeling function, (7) for each h ∈ H, ports( h) is an ordered list of distinct vertices of length rank( λ(h)), and (8) ι is an ordered list of distinct vertices in V .The ordered list ι is called the interface of G. A graph pattern with variable hyperedges is said to be ground if H = ∅. In this case, it is identified with the graph with interface ( V, E, ϕ, ψ, ι ).

Example 1. We give an example of a graph pattern with variable hyper-edges in Fig. 1. A variable hyperedge is drawn as a box with lines to its ports. The order of the ports is indicated by the digits attached to these lines. The vertices v1, v 2, v 3 form the interface of the graph pattern. 

Definition 6 (Star graph pattern) . A graph pattern with variable hyper-edges G = ( V, E, H, ϕ, ψ, λ, ports , ι ) is called a star graph pattern if (1) E = ∅,(2) H = {h} for some hyperedge h, and (3) the interface ι coincides with ports( h). 

Definition 7 (Isomorphism of graph patterns) . Let G and G′ be graph patterns with variable hyperedges, where G = ( V, E, H, ϕ, ψ, λ, ports , ι ) and 

G′ = ( V ′, E ′, H ′, ϕ ′, ψ ′, λ ′, ports ′, ι ′). An isomorphism from G to G′ is a pair (π, ρ ) consisting of bijections π : V → V ′ and ρ : H → H′ such that (1) {u, v } ∈ E if and only if {π(u), π (v)} ∈ E′,9Figure 1: A graph pattern G = ( V, E, H, φ, ψ, λ, ports, ι ) with variable hyperedges. In this example, E = ∅, H = {h1, h 2, h 3}, λ(hi) = xi for 

i = 1 , 2, 3, and ι = ( v1, v 2, v 3). The port order of each hyperedge is indicated by the digits attached to the incident lines; in particular, each variable xi

has rank 3. (2) ϕ(v) = ϕ′(π(v)) for every v ∈ V ,(3) ψ({u, v }) = ψ′({π(u), π (v)}) for every {u, v } ∈ E,(4) λ(h) = λ′(ρ(h)) for every h ∈ H,(5) if ports( h) = ( v1, . . . , v r), then ports ′(ρ(h)) = ( π(v1), . . . , π (vr)) ,

(6) if ι = ( u1, . . . , u m) and ι′ = ( u′

> 1

, . . . , u ′

> m

), then π(ui) = u′ 

> i

(i =1, . . . , m ).

If there exists an isomorphism from G to G′, then we write G ∼= G′.

Definition 8 (Binding and substitution) . Let P = ( VP , E P , ϕ P , ψ P , ι P ) be a ground graph pattern with interface, and let x ∈ X with rank( x) = |ιP |.A binding for x is an expression x := P. 

Let G = ( V, E, H, ϕ, ψ, λ, ports , ι ) be a graph pattern with variable hy-peredges, and let h ∈ H satisfy λ(h) = x. The graph pattern obtained by replacing h with P is denoted by G[h ←x P ], and is defined by removing 

h, taking a fresh copy of P , and identifying the i-th vertex of ports( h) with the i-th interface vertex of the copied P for each i = 1 , . . . , rank( x). If G contains several hyperedges labeled with the same variable x, then a binding x := P is applied simultaneously to all such hyperedges. In other words, all occurrences of the same variable label are replaced by isomorphic copies of the same ground graph pattern P .A substitution is a finite set θ = {x1 := P1, . . . , x m := Pm} such that the variables x1, . . . , x m are pairwise distinct and each Pi is a ground graph 10 pattern with interface satisfying rank( xi) = |ιPi |. For a graph pattern G,we write Gθ for the graph pattern obtained by applying all bindings in θ

simultaneously. 

2.3 Clause Systems with Fixed Interfaces 

We now introduce the clause-system formalism used in the learning algo-rithm. The graph patterns defined in the previous subsection provide the local building blocks of clauses. Variable hyperedges indicate positions at which graphs with interfaces may later be substituted. The role of fixed-interface clauses is to make this substitution structure explicit. In each clause, variable hyperedges occurring in the head pattern are linked to star graph patterns in the body. Thus the body atoms specify the subproblems associated with the interfaces of the variable hyperedges. This formulation is tailored to the later learning construction, where con-texts are represented by ordered boundary representations and candidate clauses are tested by membership queries. 

Definition 9 (Atom and clause) . Let Π be a finite set of predicate sym-bols, and let irank : Π → N be an interface-rank function. An atom is an expression of the form p(G), where p ∈ Π and G is a graph pattern with variable hyperedges such that |ι| = irank( p), where ι is the interface of G.For an atom p(G) and a substitution θ, we define p(G)θ := p(Gθ ).

A clause is an expression of the form A ← B1, . . . , B m, where m ≥ 0 and 

A, B 1, . . . , B m are atoms. The atom A is called the head of the clause, and the sequence B1, . . . , B m is called the body of the clause. If m = 0, then the clause is called a fact . A finite set of clauses is called a graph pattern clause system .

Definition 10 (Fixed-interface clause) . A clause A ← B1, . . . , B m is called a fixed-interface clause if the following conditions hold: (1) the head pattern of A is a graph pattern with variable hyperedges; (2) every body atom has a star graph pattern; (3) for each variable hyperedge occurrence h in the head pattern of A,there exists exactly one body atom whose graph pattern is a star graph pattern 

Gh = ( Vh, E h, H h, ϕ h, ψ h, λ h, ports h, ι h)with Hh = {h′} satisfying 

λh(h′) = λ(h)11 Figure 2: A fixed-interface clause system with repeated variable labels. and 

|ιh| = rank( λ(h)) .

Conversely, every body atom arises in this way from exactly one vari-able hyperedge occurrence in the head pattern. 

Example 2. Figure 2 shows a fixed-interface clause system in which the same variable label may occur in several variable hyperedges. In such a clause, all occurrences of the same variable label are replaced by isomorphic copies of the same graph with interface under a substitution. 

Definition 11 (Fixed-interface clause system (FICS)) . A graph pattern clause system is called a fixed-interface clause system if every clause in it is a fixed-interface clause. 

Remark 3. The fixed-interface clause systems considered in this paper make explicit, in graph-with-interface notation, the structural ingredients already present in regular FGSs: designated interface vertices, star-shaped body patterns, and the correspondence between variable hyperedges in the head pattern and body atoms through variable labels. In particular, every regular FGS rule with unary predicate symbols can be viewed as a fixed-interface clause of the kind considered here, after mak-ing its interface vertices and port order explicit. Conversely, fixed-interface clauses satisfying the corresponding regular-FGS syntactic restrictions give regular-FGS-style rules. The present paper, however, uses the fixed-interface formulation as the primary formalism, since the learning argument depends 12 only on the interface structure, bounded clause shape, and membership-query filtering, and not on the full regular-FGS presentation. 

Definition 12 (Derivation) . Let Γ = {C1, . . . , C t} be a graph pattern clause system. A goal is an expression of the form ← B1, . . . , B m, where m ≥ 0and B1, . . . , B m are atoms. The case m = 0 is called the empty goal .Let D =← A1, . . . , A k be a nonempty goal, and let C = A ← B1, . . . , B q

be a clause in Γ. Suppose that Aj = p(G) and A = p(H) for some j ∈{1, . . . , k }, and that there exists a substitution θ such that Hθ ∼= G. Then we say that the goal D′ =← A1, . . . , A j−1, B 1θ, . . . , B qθ, A j+1 , . . . , A k is obtained from D by one derivation step using C at Aj .A derivation from a goal D0 is a finite or infinite sequence D0, D 1, D 2, . . . 

such that each Di+1 is obtained from Di by one derivation step. A finite derivation ending with the empty goal is called a refutation .

Definition 13 (Generated language) . Let Γ be a graph pattern clause sys-tem, and let p be a unary predicate symbol. The language generated by (Γ , p ) is defined by 

L(Γ , p ) = 

(

G G is a graph and 

← p(G) has a refutation in Γ 

)

.

Definition 14 (Bounded FICS) . Let m, s, t, w, d be nonnegative integers. A fixed-interface clause system Γ is called an ( m, s, t, w, d )-bounded fixed-interface clause system if the following conditions hold: (1) the number of clauses in Γ is at most m,(2) for every variable label x occurring in Γ, we have rank( x) ≤ w,(3) for every clause A ← B1, . . . , B ℓ in Γ, (a) the number of variable hyperedges in the head pattern of A is at most s,(b) the number ℓ of body atoms is at most t,(c) every graph pattern occurring in the clause has maximum vertex degree at most d.

Definition 15 (Generated graph class) . Let m, s, t, w, d, ∆ be nonnegative integers. We denote by FICSL ∆(m, s, t, w, d ) the class of all graph lan-guages L(Γ , p ) such that (1) Γ is an ( m, s, t, w, d )-bounded fixed-interface clause system, 13 (2) p is a unary predicate symbol of Γ, (3) every graph in L(Γ , p ) has maximum degree at most ∆. 

Definition 16 (Membership problem) . Let Γ be a fixed-interface clause system and let p be a unary predicate symbol of Γ. The membership problem for (Γ , p ) is the following decision problem: 

Membership Problem for (Γ , p )

Instance: A graph G.

Question: Does G belong to L(Γ , p ) ? 

Proposition 1 (Membership bound) . Let Γ be an (m, s, t, w, d )-bounded fixed-interface clause system, and let p be a unary predicate symbol of Γ.Assume that every graph in L(Γ , p ) has maximum degree at most ∆. Then the membership problem for (Γ , p ) is solvable in 

O



ξ(m, s, t, w, ∆) n2s(w+1) · ISO( n)



,

where n is the number of vertices of the input graph, ξ is a function de-pending only on m, s, t, w, ∆, and ISO( n) denotes the time required to test isomorphism, preserving vertex labels, edge labels, and interfaces, of two graphs with interface on at most n vertices. Proof. The corresponding membership bound in [8] is stated for the class 

VH -FGSL k, ∆(m, s, t, r, w, d ). However, the counting argument used there depends on the bounded-degree assumption and on the bounded ranks of interfaces, and does not make essential use of the treewidth bound k. There-fore, in the present fixed-interface setting, we verify the required membership bound directly. Let G = ( V, E, ϕ, ψ ) be an input graph with n = |V |. We show that the membership problem for (Γ , p ) can be solved by reducing it to a finite derivation problem over suitably instantiated ground clauses. Since Γ is a fixed-interface clause system, every body atom has a star graph pattern. Thus, in a derivation of ← p(G), every variable hyperedge occurring in a head pattern is instantiated by a graph with interface. By the boundedness assumption on Γ, every variable label occurring in Γ has rank at most w. Hence, when we test membership of the input graph G, it is sufficient to consider subgraphs with interface of G whose boundary rank is at most w.Let Sub w(G) denote the set of all subgraphs with interface of G obtained from valid ordered boundary specifications of rank at most w. We estimate the size of Sub w(G). 14 Fix r ≤ w. The number of ordered boundary tuples β = ( b1, . . . , b r)of distinct vertices of G is at most nr. For a fixed such tuple, put B =

{b1, . . . , b r}. Since every graph in L(Γ , p ) has maximum degree at most ∆, we only need to consider input graphs of maximum degree at most ∆; if the input graph has maximum degree greater than ∆, then it is immediately rejected. Hence the number of edges of G incident with vertices in B is at most r∆. Therefore the number of possible boundary edge sets EB is at most 2 r∆. For each candidate pair ( β, E B ), validity can be checked and, when valid, the corresponding subgraph with interface KG(β, E B ) can be constructed. Consequently, for fixed r, the number of candidates is at most 

nr2r∆. Summing over r = 0 , 1, . . . , w , we obtain 

|Sub w(G)| ≤ 

> w

X

> r=0

nr2r∆ ≤ ξ1(w, ∆) nw+1 

for some function ξ1 depending only on w and ∆. The exponent w + 1 is a harmless coarse bound; in fact the preceding estimate gives a slightly sharper polynomial bound. Now consider a clause of Γ. By the definition of an ( m, s, t, w, d )-bounded fixed-interface clause system, the head pattern of every clause contains at most s variable hyperedges. Thus, once the underlying clause is fixed, the number of possible substitutions that replace all head variables by members of Sub w(G) is at most |Sub w(G)|s. Since Γ has at most m clauses, the total number of instantiated ground clauses that need to be considered is at most 

m |Sub w(G)|s.

From these instantiated clauses, we construct a finite ground clause set ΓG. Each atom occurring in Γ G has a ground graph with interface built from a subgraph with interface of G, and two such atoms are identified up to isomorphism preserving vertex labels, edge labels, and interfaces. Hence every required equality test between instantiated atoms can be carried out in time ISO( n). We then compute the least set of derivable ground atoms from Γ G by iteratively applying modus ponens. At each application step, one chooses a ground clause and checks whether all atoms in its body have already been derived. Since every clause body has at most t atoms, and since the total number of ground clauses is at most m|Sub w(G)|s, the total number of such checks is bounded by ξ2(m, s, t ) |Sub w(G)|2s for some function ξ2 depending only on m, s, t . Each check requires only isomorphism tests on graphs with interface of size at most n, and therefore costs at most ISO( n) time. 15 Consequently, the whole derivation procedure is completed in time 

O␀ξ2(m, s, t ) |Sub w(G)|2s · ISO( n) .

Substituting the bound |Sub w(G)| ≤ ξ1(w, ∆) nw+1 gives 

O



ξ(m, s, t, w, ∆) n2s(w+1) · ISO( n)



,

where ξ is a function depending only on m, s, t, w, ∆. Finally, ← p(G) is refutable in Γ if and only if the corresponding ground atom is derivable from Γ G. Hence the membership problem for (Γ , p ) is solvable within the claimed time bound. 

Corollary 2. Let s, w, ∆ be fixed constants. Then the membership problem for (Γ , p ) is solvable in time polynomial in m, t, and the size of the input graph. Proof. By Proposition 1, the membership problem is solvable in 

O



ξ(m, s, t, w, ∆) n2s(w+1) · ISO( n)



.

Since s, w, ∆ are fixed, the exponent 2 s(w + 1) is fixed. Moreover, the graphs with interface compared in the isomorphism tests have maximum degree bounded in terms of ∆ and the fixed clause parame-ters. Graph isomorphism for graphs of bounded valence is solvable in poly-nomial time by Luks’ algorithm [5]. Since the interfaces are ordered and have bounded rank, requiring the isomorphism to preserve interfaces only adds a fixed-size constraint and does not change the polynomial-time bound. Therefore ISO( n) is polynomially bounded in n under the present bounded-degree and bounded-interface assumptions. Hence the membership problem is solvable in time polynomial in m, t, and the size of the input graph. 

# 3 Learning Model 

We now fix the learning model. The learner receives a positive presentation of an unknown target language and has access to membership queries for that language. Its task is to output a sequence of fixed-interface clause systems that eventually generate exactly the target language. We also use the standard polynomial-time update requirement, measured with respect to the cumulative size of the observed positive examples. 16 Definition 17 (Positive presentation) . Let L be a graph language, that is, a set of graphs. An infinite sequence G1, G 2, . . . of graphs is called a positive presentation of L if every Gi belongs to L and every graph in L appears at least once in the sequence. 

Definition 18 (Membership query) . Let L be a graph language. A mem-bership query for L is a query of the form “Is G ∈ L?” where G is a graph. The answer is either yes or no .

Definition 19 (Learner) . A learner is an algorithm that, given a finite initial segment G1, . . . , G n of a positive presentation of a target language 

L∗ and access to membership queries for L∗, outputs a hypothesis (Γ n, p n), where Γ n is a fixed-interface clause system and pn is a predicate symbol of Γn.

Definition 20 (Identification in the limit) . Let C be a class of graph lan-guages. We say that C is identifiable in the limit from positive data and membership queries if there exists a learner A such that, for every target language L∗ ∈ C and every positive presentation G1, G 2, . . . of L∗, the se-quence of hypotheses (Γ 1, p 1), (Γ 2, p 2), . . . output by A satisfies the following condition: there exists a positive integer N such that L(Γ n, p n) = L∗ for all 

n ≥ N .

Definition 21 (Polynomial update) . A learner A is said to have polynomial-time update if there exists a polynomial P such that, for every target lan-guage L∗, every positive presentation G1, G 2, . . . , and every n ≥ 1, the time required by A to output (Γ n, p n) after reading G1, . . . , G n is at most 

P (Pni=1 |V (Gi)|) .

# 4 Main Results 

4.1 Learning algorithm 

Before presenting the algorithm, we explain the role of the two finite sets 

F and R. Both consist of ordered boundary representations extracted from the positive examples observed so far. The set F serves as the predicate basis of the current hypothesis: for each ρ ∈ F ∪ { ρ∅}, the construction introduces a predicate symbol pρ. The set R is used as a residual test set for clause admission. For an ordered boundary representation ρ, we write can( ρ) for the cor-responding graph with interface. The observation table records, for ρ ∈ F 

17 and σ ∈ R , whether can( ρ) ⊙ can( σ) belongs to the target language. Us-ing this table and membership queries, the learner constructs Γ( F, R) by admitting exactly those bounded fixed-interface clause candidates that pass the clause-admission test. Algorithm 4 updates F only when the current hypothesis fails to cover the observed positive examples, while R is recomputed from the current sample at every stage. Thus even when the predicate basis is unchanged, the hypothesis may change because the clause-admission tests are re-executed against the enlarged residual set. 

Definition 22 (Composition along interfaces) . Let G = ( VG, E G, ϕ G, ψ G, ι G)and H = ( VH , E H , ϕ H , ψ H , ι H ) be graphs with interface. Assume that 

|ιG| = |ιH |. The composition of G and H along their interfaces, denoted by G ⊙ H, is defined as follows. Take disjoint copies of G and H. Let ιG = ( u1, . . . , u r) and ιH =(v1, . . . , v r). For each i = 1 , . . . , r , identify the vertex ui with the vertex 

vi. The resulting labeled graph is denoted by G ⊙ H.If |ιG|̸ = |ιH |, then G ⊙ H is undefined. 

Remark 4. In the composition G ⊙ H, the interface vertices are used only for gluing. Hence the result is regarded as a graph with interface only when an interface is specified separately. In particular, when we use G ⊙ H in the definitions of contexts and substructures below, it will usually be treated simply as a graph. 

Definition 23 (Context of a predicate) . Let Γ be a fixed-interface clause system, let p be the designated start predicate symbol of Γ, let q be a unary predicate symbol of Γ, and let G be a graph with interface. We say that G

is a context of q with respect to (Γ , p ) if, for every graph with interface K,

← q(K) has a refutation in Γ 

⇐⇒ ← p(G ⊙ K) has a refutation in Γ ,

whenever the composition is defined. 

Definition 24 (Finite context property) . Let Γ be a fixed-interface clause system, and let p be a unary predicate symbol of Γ with irank( p) = 0. We say that (Γ , p ) has the finite context property if, for every unary predicate symbol q of Γ, there exists a graph with interface Gq such that Gq is a context of q with respect to (Γ , p ). 

Definition 25 (Context-learnable graph languages) . Let m, s, t, w, d, ∆ be nonnegative integers. We denote by FICSL FCP ∆ (m, s, t, w, d ) the class of all graph languages L(Γ , p ) such that 18 (1) Γ is an ( m, s, t, w, d )-bounded fixed-interface clause system, (2) p is a unary predicate symbol of Γ, (3) (Γ , p ) has the finite context property, (4) every graph in L(Γ , p ) has maximum degree at most ∆. 

Remark 5 (Degree-safe clause systems) . A sufficient condition for the bounded-degree requirement in the above definition is the following. Call a fixed-interface clause A ← B1, . . . , B m degree-safe if every vertex occurring in the port list of some hyperedge in the head pattern of A belongs to ex-actly one such port list and is incident with no ordinary edge of the head pattern. A fixed-interface clause system is degree-safe if every clause in it is degree-safe. If Γ is degree-safe, then the degree of every vertex in any graph in L(Γ , p ) is bounded by the maximum vertex degree appearing in the head patterns of clauses of Γ, which is a finite constant depending only on Γ. 

Example 3. Figure 3 illustrates the finite context property for a simple fixed-interface clause system. The start predicate is p, with irank( p) = 0, while q and r are auxiliary unary predicate symbols with interface rank 2. The predicate r generates path-like interface graphs, q combines such inter-face graphs, and p derives closed graphs from q-structures. For each auxil-iary predicate s ∈ { q, r }, the derivability of s(K) can be tested, for every graph with interface K, by placing K into a suitable context Cs and asking whether the resulting closed graph is derivable from the start predicate p.Thus the auxiliary predicates are represented by contexts with respect to p,as required by the finite context property. 

Definition 26 (Context candidates) . Let D be a nonempty finite set of graphs. We define Con( D) to be the set of all graphs with interface G such that there exists a graph with interface H satisfying G ⊙ H ∈ D. 

Definition 27 (Characteristic sample) . Let (Γ , p ) be a fixed-interface clause system with the finite context property. A finite subset D ⊆ L(Γ , p ) is called 

characteristic for contexts if, for every unary predicate symbol q of Γ, the set Con( D) contains a context of q with respect to (Γ , p ). 

Theorem 1 (Identification in the limit) . For every target language L∗ ∈FICSL FCP ∆ (m, s, t, w, d ), Algorithm 4 identifies L∗ in the limit from positive data and membership queries. 

Remark 6. The proof of the theorem will be given stepwise. The main ingredients are: 19 Figure 3: A fixed-interface clause system illustrating the finite context property. The start predicate p has interface rank 0, whereas the auxiliary predicates q and r have interface rank 2. (1) every target context eventually appears in Con( Dn), (2) the hypothesis eventually contains predicate representatives correspond-ing to all target predicates, (3) every target clause is eventually reconstructed in Γ( F, R), and (4) spurious clauses are excluded by membership queries. Let G∅ := ( ∅, ∅, ϕ ∅, ψ ∅, ()) denote the empty graph with empty interface, and let p∅ be the unary predicate symbol corresponding to G∅.

Definition 28 (Realization of a head pattern) . Let H be a graph pattern with variable hyperedges, where H = ( V, E, eH, ϕ, ψ, λ, ports , ι ), and let θ =

{x1 := K1, . . . , x m := Km} be a substitution, where each Ki is a graph with interface and rank( xi) = |ιKi |. Assume that every variable label occurring in H belongs to {x1, . . . , x m}.The realization of H by θ, denoted by Real( H, θ ), is the graph with interface obtained from H by simultaneously replacing every hyperedge la-beled xi by a fresh copy of Ki, identifying the j-th vertex of the port list of that hyperedge with the j-th interface vertex of the copy of Ki for each 

j = 1 , . . . , rank( xi), and finally keeping the interface ι of H as the interface of the resulting graph. Equivalently, if Hθ denotes the graph pattern obtained by applying θ to 

H, then Real( H, θ ) is the graph with interface underlying Hθ .20 Algorithm 1 Construction of ordered boundary representations 

Input: A finite positive sample D and an interface-rank bound w

Output: A finite set BRep w(D) of ordered boundary representations  

> 1:

BRep w(D) ← ∅  

> 2:

for all X = ( VX , E X , ϕ X , ψ X , ()) ∈ D do  

> 3:

for r = 0 , 1, . . . , w do  

> 4:

Br(X) ← { (b1, . . . , b r) | b1, . . . , b r are distinct vertices of X} 

> 5:

for all β = ( b1, . . . , b r) ∈ B r(X) do  

> 6:

B ← { b1, . . . , b r} 

> 7:

Inc X (B) ← { e ∈ EX | e ∩ B̸ = ∅}  

> 8:

for all EB ⊆ Inc X (B) do  

> 9:

if (β, E B ) is valid in X then  

> 10:

K ← KX (β, E B ) 

> 11:

ρ ← (β, E B ) 

> 12:

can( ρ) ← K 

> 13:

Add ρ to BRep w(D) 

> 14:

end if  

> 15:

end for  

> 16:

end for  

> 17:

end for  

> 18:

end for  

> 19:

return BRep w(D)Throughout Algorithms 1–4, we write Oracle L∗ (G) for the result of the membership query “ G ∈ L∗?”, which returns true or false .For an ordered boundary representation ρ, we write rank( ρ) for the length of its ordered boundary. Whenever a representative graph with inter-face is needed, we write can( ρ) for the subgraph with interface determined by ρ.

Remark 7. At each stage n, the clause set Γ( F, R) is recomputed from the current sets F and R. In particular, even when F is not updated, the residual set R is updated to BRep w(Dn), and the observation table Obs F,R

and the clause-admission tests in Algorithm 3 are recomputed with respect to the new R. Thus the hypothesis at stage n is determined by the current pair ( F, R), not merely by the current predicate basis F.21 Algorithm 2 Construction of the observation table Obs F,R

Input: Finite sets F and R of ordered boundary representations 

Output: A Boolean table Obs F,R on F × R  

> 1:

Initialize Obs F,R(ρ, σ ) ← false for all ( ρ, σ ) ∈ F × R  

> 2:

for all ρ ∈ F do  

> 3:

for all σ ∈ R do  

> 4:

Let G ← can( ρ) and K ← can( σ) 

> 5:

if G ⊙ K is defined and Oracle L∗ (G ⊙ K) then  

> 6:

Obs F,R(ρ, σ ) ← true  

> 7:

end if  

> 8:

end for  

> 9:

end for  

> 10:

return Obs F,R

4.2 Identification in the limit 

We now prove Theorem 1. The proof is organized around the eventual ap-pearance of target contexts and the eventual elimination of spurious clauses. First, we show that every relevant target context eventually appears among the ordered boundary representations extracted from the observed positive examples. This yields predicate representatives for all target pred-icates at sufficiently late update stages. Next, we prove that every target clause is then reconstructed over these representatives, and that refutations in the target system can be simulated in the reconstructed hypothesis. The remaining part of the proof establishes eventual soundness. Once the predicate basis is fixed, every spurious non-fact clause is eventually exposed by some residual representative and is rejected by a membership query. Hence, after sufficiently many examples, the hypotheses generate no graph outside the target language. Combining this eventual soundness with the simulation of target refutations gives identification in the limit. 

Lemma 5. Assume that L∗ = L(Γ ∗, p ∗) ∈ FICSL FCP ∆ (m, s, t, w, d ), and let G1, G 2, . . . be a positive presentation of L∗. For each n ≥ 1, let Dn =

{G1, . . . , G n}. Let q be a unary predicate symbol of Γ∗ such that there exists a graph with interface Kq for which ← q(Kq) has a refutation in Γ∗. Then there exist a context Cq of q with respect to (Γ ∗, p ∗) and a positive integer 

Nq such that Cq ∈ Con( Dn) for every n ≥ Nq.Proof. Since (Γ ∗, p ∗) has the finite context property, there exists a graph with interface Cq that is a context of q with respect to (Γ ∗, p ∗). 22 Algorithm 3 Construction of Γ( F, R)

Input: Finite sets F and R of ordered boundary representations 

Output: A fixed-interface clause system Γ( F, R) 

> 1:

Γ( F, R) ← ∅  

> 2:

for all ρ ∈ F ∪ { ρ∅} do  

> 3:

Introduce pρ with irank( pρ) = rank( ρ) 

> 4:

end for  

> 5:

Obs F,R ← ConstructObservationTable( F, R) 

> 6:

C ← { pρ(H) ← pρ1 (H1), . . . , p ρℓ (Hℓ) | ρ, ρ 1, . . . , ρ ℓ ∈ F ∪ { ρ∅} } 

where 0 ≤ ℓ ≤ t and the bounds s, w, d are satisfied  

> 7:

for all C = pρ(H) ← pρ1 (H1), . . . , p ρℓ (Hℓ) ∈ C do  

> 8:

if ℓ = 0 then  

> 9:

Let G ← can( ρ) 

> 10:

if G ⊙ H is defined and Oracle L∗ (G ⊙ H) then  

> 11:

Add C to Γ( F, R) 

> 12:

end if  

> 13:

else  

> 14:

Let xi be the variable label of Hi for each i = 1 , . . . , ℓ  

> 15:

Let X(C) = {xi | 1 ≤ i ≤ ℓ} 

> 16:

admissible ← true  

> 17:

for all (σx)x∈X(C) ∈ R X(C) do  

> 18:

if Obs F,R(ρi, σ xi ) = true for all i = 1 , . . . , ℓ then  

> 19:

Let Kx ← can( σx) for each x ∈ X(C) 

> 20:

if K ← Real ␀H, {x := Kx}x∈X(C)

 is defined then  

> 21:

if ¬Oracle L∗

␀can( ρ) ⊙ K then  

> 22:

admissible ← false  

> 23:

break  

> 24:

end if  

> 25:

end if  

> 26:

end if  

> 27:

end for  

> 28:

if admissible then  

> 29:

Add C to Γ( F, R) 

> 30:

end if  

> 31:

end if  

> 32:

end for  

> 33:

return Γ( F, R)23 Algorithm 4 Learning algorithm 

Input: A positive presentation G1, G 2, . . . of the target language L∗, an interface-rank bound w, and access to membership queries for L∗

Output: A sequence of hypotheses (Γ n, p ρ∅ ) 

> 1:

F ← ∅  

> 2:

R ← ∅  

> 3:

for n = 1 , 2, 3, . . . do  

> 4:

Dn ← { G1, . . . , G n} 

> 5:

bΓ ← ConstructGamma( F, R) // uses Oracle L∗ 

> 6:

if Dn ⊈ L(bΓ, p ρ∅ ) then  

> 7:

F ← BRep w(Dn) 

> 8:

end if  

> 9:

R ← BRep w(Dn) 

> 10:

Γn ← ConstructGamma( F, R) // uses Oracle L∗ 

> 11:

Output (Γ n, p ρ∅ ) 

> 12:

end for 

By assumption, ← q(Kq) has a refutation in Γ ∗. Since Cq is a context of 

q with respect to (Γ ∗, p ∗), it follows from Definition 23 that ← p∗(Cq ⊙ Kq)also has a refutation in Γ ∗. Hence Cq ⊙ Kq ∈ L∗.

Since G1, G 2, . . . is a positive presentation of L∗, there exists a positive integer Nq such that Cq ⊙ Kq ∈ Dn for every n ≥ Nq. By Definition 26, this implies Cq ∈ Con( Dn) for every n ≥ Nq.

Lemma 6. Assume that L∗ = L(Γ ∗, p ∗) ∈ FICSL FCP ∆ (m, s, t, w, d ), and let G1, G 2, . . . be a positive presentation of L∗. For each n ≥ 1, let Dn =

{G1, . . . , G n}. Let q be a unary predicate symbol of Γ∗ such that there exists a graph with interface Kq for which ← q(Kq) has a refutation in Γ∗.Then there exist a context Cq of q with respect to (Γ ∗, p ∗), an ordered boundary representation ρq, and a positive integer Nq such that, for every 

n ≥ Nq, ρq ∈ BRep w(Dn) and can( ρq) = Cq.

Proof. By Lemma 5, there exist a context Cq of q with respect to (Γ ∗, p ∗)and a positive integer Nq such that Cq ∈ Con( Dn) for every n ≥ Nq.Fix n ≥ Nq. Since Cq ∈ Con( Dn), there exists a graph with interface 

Hq such that Cq ⊙ Hq ∈ Dn. Let X = Cq ⊙ Hq. In the composition X, the graph with interface Cq occurs as a subgraph with interface of X, whose ordered boundary is the interface of Cq. Let ρq be the ordered boundary representation of this subgraph with interface. 24 Since Cq has interface rank at most w, Algorithm 1 enumerates ρq when it is applied to Dn. Hence ρq ∈ BRep w(Dn). Moreover, by the construction of Algorithm 1, the canonical representative associated with ρq is precisely the subgraph with interface represented by ρq. Therefore can( ρq) = Cq. This proves the lemma. 

Definition 29 (Update stage) . Let (Γ n, p ρ∅ ) be the n-th hypothesis output by Algorithm 4. We say that n is an update stage if Algorithm 4 executes the assignment F ← BRep w(Dn) at stage n.

Lemma 7. Assume that L∗ = L(Γ ∗, p ∗) ∈ FICSL FCP ∆ (m, s, t, w, d ), and let G1, G 2, . . . be a positive presentation of L∗. For each n ≥ 1, let Dn =

{G1, . . . , G n}. If Dn is characteristic for contexts with respect to (Γ ∗, p ∗) and 

n is an update stage, then for every unary predicate symbol q of Γ∗ such that there exists a graph with interface Kq for which ← q(Kq) has a refutation in Γ∗, the hypothesis (Γ n, p ρ∅ ) contains a predicate symbol pρq such that 

can( ρq) = Cq, where Cq is a context of q with respect to (Γ ∗, p ∗).Proof. Since Dn is characteristic for contexts, Definition 27 implies that, for every such unary predicate symbol q, the set Con( Dn) contains a context 

Cq of q with respect to (Γ ∗, p ∗). By the preceding lemma, there exists an ordered boundary representa-tion ρq ∈ BRep w(Dn) such that can( ρq) = Cq. Since n is an update stage, Algorithm 4 sets F = BRep w(Dn) at stage n. Hence ρq ∈ F .

By Algorithm 3, for each ρ ∈ F ∪ { ρ∅}, the construction introduces a predicate symbol pρ with irank( pρ) = rank( ρ). Therefore pρq occurs in Γn.

Definition 30 (Spurious clause candidate) . Let 

C = pρ(H) ← pρ1 (H1), . . . , p ρℓ (Hℓ)be a fixed-interface clause candidate with ℓ ≥ 1. For each i = 1 , . . . , ℓ , let 

xi be the unique variable label occurring in the star graph pattern Hi, and put X(C) = {xi | 1 ≤ i ≤ ℓ}. We say that C is spurious with respect to L∗ if there exist graphs with interface Kx (x ∈ X(C)) such that can( ρi) ⊙ Kxi ∈

L∗ (i = 1 , . . . , ℓ ), the realization Real ␀H, {x := Kx}x∈X(C)

 is defined, and can( ρ) ⊙ Real ␀H, {x := Kx}x∈X(C)

 /∈ L∗. If no such family ( Kx)x∈X(C)

exists, then C is called non-spurious with respect to L∗.

Lemma 8. Let C = pρ(H) ← pρ1 (H1), . . . , p ρℓ (Hℓ) be a fixed-interface clause candidate with ℓ ≥ 1. For each i = 1 , . . . , ℓ , let xi be the unique 

25 variable label occurring in the star graph pattern Hi, and put X(C) = {xi |

1 ≤ i ≤ ℓ}. Assume that C is non-spurious with respect to L∗.Let (Kx)x∈X(C) be a family of graphs with interface such that can( ρi) ⊙

Kxi ∈ L∗ (i = 1 , . . . , ℓ ). Assume that Real ␀H, {x := Kx}x∈X(C)

 is defined. Then can( ρ) ⊙ Real ␀H, {x := Kx}x∈X(C)

 ∈ L∗.

Proof. Assume, toward a contradiction, that can( ρ) ⊙ Real ␀H, {x := Kx}x∈X(C)

 /∈ L∗.

Then the family ( Kx)x∈X(C) witnesses that C is spurious with respect to 

L∗, because can( ρi) ⊙ Kxi ∈ L∗ (i = 1 , . . . , ℓ ), while can( ρ) ⊙ Real ␀H, {x := 

Kx}x∈X(C)

 /∈ L∗. This contradicts the assumption that C is non-spurious. Therefore can( ρ) ⊙ Real ␀H, {x := Kx}x∈X(C)

 ∈ L∗.

Lemma 9. Assume that L∗ = L(Γ ∗, p ∗) ∈ FICSL FCP ∆ (m, s, t, w, d ), and let G1, G 2, . . . be a positive presentation of L∗. For each n ≥ 1, let Dn =

{G1, . . . , G n}.

Then there exists an integer N1 such that, for every update stage N ≥

N1, the hypothesis (Γ N , p ρ∅ ) contains predicate symbols corresponding to all target predicate contexts, and every target clause of Γ∗ is reconstructed in 

ΓN as a clause over those predicate representatives. Proof. By Lemma 5, for every unary predicate symbol q of Γ ∗ for which there exists a graph with interface Kq such that ← q(Kq) has a refutation in Γ ∗, there exist a context Cq of q with respect to (Γ ∗, p ∗) and an integer 

Nq such that Cq ∈ Con( Dn) for all n ≥ Nq.Since Γ ∗ has only finitely many predicate symbols, we may choose an integer N1 such that, for all n ≥ N1, the sample Dn is characteristic for contexts. Let N ≥ N1 be an update stage. Then, by Lemma 7, for every relevant target predicate symbol q of Γ ∗, the hypothesis (Γ N , p ρ∅ ) contains a predicate symbol pρq such that can( ρq) = Cq, where Cq is the chosen context of q with respect to (Γ ∗, p ∗). Now let q(H) ← q1(H1), . . . , q ℓ(Hℓ) be any clause of Γ ∗, where 0 ≤ ℓ ≤ t.For each predicate symbol qi, let Cqi be the chosen context of qi, and let ρqi

be its corresponding representative in F, so that can( ρqi ) = Cqi . Similarly, let ρq ∈ F satisfy can( ρq) = Cq.

26 Since N is an update stage, all these representatives belong to the current predicate basis F. Hence Algorithm 3 considers the bounded fixed-interface clause candidate pρq (H) ← pρq1 (H1), . . . , p ρqℓ (Hℓ).

It remains to show that this candidate is admitted into Γ N .First suppose that ℓ = 0. Then the target clause is q(H) ← . Hence 

← q(H) has a refutation in Γ ∗. Since Cq is a context of q with respect to (Γ ∗, p ∗), we have Cq ⊙ H ∈ L∗. Equivalently, can( ρq) ⊙ H ∈ L∗. Therefore the membership query in the ℓ = 0 case of Algorithm 3 returns true , and the candidate clause pρq (H) ← is added to Γ N .Next suppose that ℓ ≥ 1. For each i = 1 , . . . , ℓ , let xi be the unique variable label occurring in the star graph pattern Hi, and put X = {xi | 1 ≤

i ≤ ℓ}. To prove that the candidate is admitted, it is enough to verify the non-fact admission condition of Algorithm 3. Thus we consider an arbitrary family ( σx)x∈X ∈ R X for which all body observations are positive. If no such family exists, the admission condition is satisfied vacuously. Assume therefore that Obs F,R(ρqi , σ xi ) = true for all i = 1 , . . . , ℓ . By the definition of the observation table, this means that can( ρqi ) ⊙ can( σxi ) ∈ L∗

for all i = 1 , . . . , ℓ . Since can( ρqi ) = Cqi , we have Cqi ⊙ can( σxi ) ∈ L∗ for all 

i = 1 , . . . , ℓ .Because Cqi is a context of qi with respect to (Γ ∗, p ∗), it follows that 

← qi(can( σxi )) has a refutation in Γ ∗ for every i = 1 , . . . , ℓ .Now let θ = {x := can( σx)}x∈X . This is a single substitution on variable labels. Hence if the same variable label occurs in several body atoms or several hyperedges of the head pattern, all such occurrences are replaced by the same graph with interface. Assume that K = Real( H, θ ) is defined. Applying the target clause q(H) ← q1(H1), . . . , q ℓ(Hℓ) in Γ ∗ with the substitution θ, and using the refutations of the body goals obtained above, we obtain a refutation of ← q(K) in Γ ∗. Since Cq is a context of q with re-spect to (Γ ∗, p ∗), this implies Cq ⊙ K ∈ L∗. Equivalently, can( ρq) ⊙ K ∈ L∗.

Thus every membership query required by the non-fact admission test in Al-gorithm 3 is answered positively. Therefore the candidate clause pρq (H) ←

pρq1 (H1), . . . , p ρqℓ (Hℓ) is admitted into Γ N .Since the target clause was arbitrary, every target clause of Γ ∗ is re-constructed in Γ N over the predicate representatives given by the target contexts. 

Lemma 10. Assume the same setting as in Lemma 9. Let N be an update stage at which every target clause is reconstructed in ΓN . If ← q(K) has a refutation in Γ∗, then ← pρq (K) has a refutation in ΓN , where pρq is the predicate representative of q used in ΓN .

27 Proof. We prove the claim by induction on the refutation tree in Γ ∗. Con-sider the first target clause applied in the refutation. By Lemma 9, this clause has a reconstructed counterpart in Γ N , obtained by replacing each target predicate symbol q′ with its corresponding predicate representative 

pρq′ . The graph patterns in the head and body are unchanged. Thus the same substitution on variable labels used in the target deriva-tion step can be used in the reconstructed clause. In particular, if the same variable label occurs more than once in the clause, all its occurrences are replaced by the same graph with interface in both derivations. Hence the head instance and the body instances produced in the reconstructed clause correspond exactly to those produced in the target clause, with predicate symbols replaced by their representatives. The remaining body goals correspond to smaller sub-refutations in the original refutation tree. By the induction hypothesis, these body goals have refutations in Γ N after replacing target predicate symbols by their corre-sponding predicate representatives. Therefore the whole refutation of ← q(K) in Γ ∗ is transformed into a refutation of ← pρq (K) in Γ N .

Lemma 11. Assume that L∗ = L(Γ ∗, p ∗) ∈ FICSL FCP ∆ (m, s, t, w, d ). Let 

M be a stage such that the set F is not updated after stage M . Then there exists a stage N0 ≥ M such that, for every n ≥ N0, L(Γ n, p ρ∅ ) ⊆ L∗.

Proof. Let F∗ be the fixed value of F after stage M . Consider all bounded fixed-interface clause candidates over F∗ ∪{ ρ∅} with bounds s, w, d and with at most t body atoms, as considered in Algorithm 3. Since F∗ is finite and the parameters s, t, w, d are fixed, there are only finitely many such clause candidates. Let S be the set of non-fact clause candidates among them that are spuri-ous with respect to L∗. Thus, for each C = pρ(H) ← pρ1 (H1), . . . , p ρℓ (Hℓ) ∈S with ℓ ≥ 1, let xi be the unique variable label occurring in the star graph pattern Hi for each i = 1 , . . . , ℓ , and put X(C) = {xi | 1 ≤ i ≤ ℓ}. Since C

is spurious, there exists a family of graphs with interface ( KCx )x∈X(C) such that can( ρi) ⊙ KCxi ∈ L∗ for all i = 1 , . . . , ℓ , the realization Real( H, {x := 

KCx }x∈X(C)) is defined, and can( ρ) ⊙ Real( H, {x := KCx }x∈X(C)) /∈ L∗.

For each C ∈ S and each x ∈ X(C), choose an index i with xi = x. Then the graph can( ρi) ⊙ KCx belongs to L∗. Hence it eventually appears in the positive presentation. When it has appeared, the graph with interface KCx

occurs as a subgraph with interface of an observed positive example. There-fore its ordered boundary representation, say σCx , is eventually included in 28 BRep w(Dn), and we may choose it so that can( σCx ) = KCx . Since S is finite, there exists N0 ≥ M such that, for every n ≥ N0, all such representatives 

σCx for all C ∈ S and all x ∈ X(C) belong to the current residual set 

R = BRep w(Dn).

Fix n ≥ N0. We first observe that no clause candidate in S is included in Γ n. Indeed, let C = pρ(H) ← pρ1 (H1), . . . , p ρℓ (Hℓ) ∈ S . Let xi be the unique variable label occurring in Hi, and put X(C) = {xi | 1 ≤ i ≤ ℓ}.Take the corresponding family ( σCx )x∈X(C) ∈ R X(C). For each i = 1 , . . . , ℓ ,we have can( ρi)⊙can( σCxi ) = can( ρi)⊙KCxi ∈ L∗. Hence the observation table satisfies Obs F,R(ρi, σ Cxi ) = true for all i = 1 , . . . , ℓ . Moreover, Real( H, {x := can( σCx )}x∈X(C)) = Real( H, {x := KCx }x∈X(C)) is defined, while can( ρ) ⊙

Real( H, {x := can( σCx )}x∈X(C)) /∈ L∗. Therefore the membership query in Algorithm 3 returns false for this family, and C is rejected. Thus no spurious non-fact candidate from S is included in Γ n.Consequently, every non-fact clause of Γ n is non-spurious with respect to L∗. In addition, every zero-body clause pρ(H) ← included in Γ n is di-rectly sound by Algorithm 3: it is added only if can( ρ) ⊙ H is defined and Oracle L∗ (can( ρ) ⊙ H) returns true .We now prove the following claim by induction on the length of a refu-tation in Γ n.If ← pρ(K) has a refutation in Γ n, then can( ρ)⊙K ∈ L∗, whenever the composition is defined. First suppose that the refutation has length one. Then the first and only clause used is a zero-body clause pρ(H) ← of Γ n. Since fixed-interface clauses have one body atom corresponding to each variable hyperedge in the head pattern, the head pattern H has no variable hyperedges when the body length is zero. Thus H is ground, and the goal refuted by this clause is ← pρ(H). Hence K = H.By the zero-body admission test in Algorithm 3, the clause was added only when can( ρ) ⊙ H is defined and Oracle L∗

␀can( ρ) ⊙ H returns true .Therefore can( ρ) ⊙ H ∈ L∗. Since K = H, we obtain can( ρ) ⊙ K ∈ L∗. Thus the claim holds in the base case. Next assume that the claim holds for all goals refutable by derivations of length less than some integer a, and suppose that ← pρ(K) has a refutation of length a in Γ n. Let the first derivation step use a non-fact clause pρ(H) ←

pρ1 (H1), . . . , p ρr (Hr) of Γ n. For each i = 1 , . . . , r , let xi be the unique variable label occurring in the star graph pattern Hi, and put X = {xi | 1 ≤

i ≤ r}.Let θ = {x := Kx}x∈X be the substitution on variable labels used in 29 the first derivation step, where ( Kx)x∈X is a family of graphs with interface, and suppose that Real( H, θ ) = K. Then each body goal ← pρi (Kxi ) has a refutation of length less than a. By the induction hypothesis, can( ρi)⊙Kxi ∈

L∗ for all i = 1 , . . . , r .Since the clause used in the first step is a non-fact clause of Γ n, it is non-spurious with respect to L∗. Therefore, by Lemma 8, can( ρ)⊙Real( H, {x := 

Kx}x∈X ) ∈ L∗. Because Real( H, {x := Kx}x∈X ) = K, we obtain can( ρ) ⊙

K ∈ L∗. This proves the induction claim. Finally, let G ∈ L(Γ n, p ρ∅ ). Then ← pρ∅ (G) has a refutation in Γ n.Applying the claim with ρ = ρ∅, we obtain can( ρ∅) ⊙ G ∈ L∗. Since can( ρ∅)is the empty graph with empty interface, we have can( ρ∅) ⊙ G = G. Hence 

G ∈ L∗.Since G ∈ L(Γ n, p ρ∅ ) was arbitrary, we conclude L(Γ n, p ρ∅ ) ⊆ L∗.

Proof of Theorem 1. Let L∗ = L(Γ ∗, p ∗) ∈ FICSL FCP ∆ (m, s, t, w, d ), and let 

G1, G 2, . . . be an arbitrary positive presentation of L∗.By Lemma 9, there exists an integer N1 such that, for every update stage 

N ≥ N1, the hypothesis (Γ N , p ρ∅ ) contains predicate representatives for all target predicate contexts, and every target clause of Γ ∗ is reconstructed in ΓN over those representatives. We distinguish two cases. First suppose that there exists an update stage M ≥ N1. Then, by Lemma 9, every target clause of Γ ∗ is reconstructed in Γ M . Hence, by Lemma 10, L∗ ⊆ L(Γ M , p ρ∅ ). Therefore DM +1 ⊆ L∗ ⊆ L(Γ M , p ρ∅ ). Since the hypothesis used in the update test at stage M + 1 is precisely the hypothesis constructed from the current pair ( F, R) at stage M , the update condition in Algorithm 4 fails at stage M + 1. Thus F is not updated at stage M + 1. Moreover, after stage M , the predicate basis F still contains the rep-resentatives of all target predicate contexts. Although the residual set R

is updated to BRep w(Dn) at each stage, the reconstructed target clauses remain admissible for every later R. Indeed, the argument in the proof of Lemma 9 shows that genuine target clauses pass the membership-query test in Algorithm 3 for every family of residual representatives for which the body observations are positive. Consequently, the same simulation argument as in Lemma 10 gives L∗ ⊆

L(Γ M +1 , p ρ∅ ). It follows that DM +2 ⊆ L∗ ⊆ L(Γ M +1 , p ρ∅ ), and hence no update is performed at stage M + 2. Repeating this argument inductively, no update is performed at any stage after M , and for every n ≥ M , L∗ ⊆ L(Γ n, p ρ∅ ). In particular, the set 

F is fixed after stage M .30 Now apply Lemma 11 to this stage M . There exists a stage N0 ≥ M

such that, for every n ≥ N0, L(Γ n, p ρ∅ ) ⊆ L∗. Combining the two inclusions, we obtain L(Γ n, p ρ∅ ) = L∗ for every n ≥ N0.It remains to consider the second case, where there is no update stage 

N ≥ N1. Then the predicate basis F is fixed from stage N1 onward. Since F is fixed and the structural parameters s, t, w, d are fixed, there are only finitely many bounded fixed-interface clause candidates over F ∪ {ρ∅}. As n increases, the residual set R = BRep w(Dn) can only grow. There-fore the clause-admission test in Algorithm 3 can only reject additional non-fact candidates; it cannot make a previously rejected candidate admissible again. The zero-body clauses are independent of R. Hence the sequence of clause sets Γ n, for n ≥ N1, is descending inside a finite set of candidates. Therefore there exists a stage M ≥ N1 such that Γ n = Γ M for every n ≥ M .We show that L∗ ⊆ L(Γ M , p ρ∅ ). Let G ∈ L∗. Since G1, G 2, . . . is a positive presentation of L∗, there exists k ≥ M + 1 such that G ∈ Dk. By the assumption of the present case, no update is performed at stage k. Hence the update condition in Algorithm 4 fails at stage k, and therefore Dk ⊆

L(Γ k−1, p ρ∅ ). Since k − 1 ≥ M , we have Γ k−1 = Γ M . Thus G ∈ L(Γ M , p ρ∅ ).

Because G ∈ L∗ was arbitrary, L∗ ⊆ L(Γ M , p ρ∅ ). Since Γ n = Γ M for all 

n ≥ M , it follows that L∗ ⊆ L(Γ n, p ρ∅ ) for every n ≥ M .On the other hand, since F is fixed from stage N1 onward, Lemma 11 gives a stage N0 ≥ N1 such that, for every n ≥ N0, L(Γ n, p ρ∅ ) ⊆ L∗. Taking 

N = max {M, N 0}, we obtain L(Γ n, p ρ∅ ) = L∗ for every n ≥ N .In both cases, there exists an integer N such that L(Γ n, p ρ∅ ) = L∗ for all n ≥ N . Hence Algorithm 4 identifies L∗ in the limit from positive data and membership queries. 

4.3 Polynomial-time update 

We next analyze the update complexity of Algorithm 4. The main point is that, under the bounded-degree assumption and the fixed interface-rank bound, only polynomially many ordered boundary representations can be extracted from the observed positive examples. The analysis proceeds as follows. We first bound the size and construc-tion time of BRep w(Dn). This immediately bounds the number of predi-cate symbols introduced from the current predicate basis. We then bound the number of bounded fixed-interface clause candidates and the number of membership queries used in the observation table and clause-admission tests. Finally, these estimates are combined with the membership bound of Proposition 1 to obtain polynomial-time update. 31 Let Sn = Pni=1 |V (Gi)| denote the cumulative size of the positive exam-ples observed up to stage n.

Lemma 12. Let Dn = {G1, . . . , G n} and let Sn = Pni=1 |V (Gi)|. As-sume that every Gi has maximum degree at most ∆. For fixed w and 

∆, the size of BRep w(Dn) is bounded by a polynomial in Sn. More pre-cisely, there exists a function ξ1, depending only on w and ∆, such that 

|BRep w(Dn)| ≤ ξ1(w, ∆) Swn . Moreover, BRep w(Dn) can be constructed in time O␀∆ 2 w∆Sw+1 

> n

 .

Proof. Fix i ∈ { 1, . . . , n }, and put ni = |V (Gi)|. For a fixed boundary rank 

r ≤ w, the number of ordered boundary tuples β = ( b1, . . . , b r) of distinct vertices of Gi is at most nri . For each such tuple, since Gi has maximum degree at most ∆, the number of edges incident with the boundary vertices is at most r∆. Hence the number of possible boundary edge sets EB is at most 2 r∆. Therefore the number of ordered boundary specifications of rank r arising from Gi is at most nri 2r∆. Summing over r = 0 , 1, . . . , w ,the number of ordered boundary representations of rank at most w arising from Gi is at most Pwr=0 nri 2r∆. Thus |BRep w(Dn)| ≤ Pni=1 

Pwr=0 nri 2r∆.

Since w and ∆ are fixed, there is a function ξ1 depending only on w and ∆ such that Pwr=0 nri 2r∆ ≤ ξ1(w, ∆) nwi for every i. Hence |BRep w(Dn)| ≤ 

ξ1(w, ∆) Pni=1 nwi . Since Pni=1 nwi ≤ (Pni=1 ni)w = Swn , we obtain 

|BRep w(Dn)| ≤ ξ1(w, ∆) Swn .

The construction time bound follows from Algorithm 1. For each can-didate ( β, E B ), validity can be tested and the corresponding subgraph with interface can be constructed in O(|V (Gi)|+|E(Gi)|) time. Since Gi has max-imum degree at most ∆, this is O(∆ ni). Therefore the total construction time is bounded by 

> n

X

> i=1

O

> w

X

> r=0

nri 2r∆ · ∆ni

!

= O ∆ 2 w∆

> n

X

> i=1

nw+1 

> i

!

.

Since Pni=1 nw+1  

> i

≤ (Pni=1 ni)w+1 = Sw+1  

> n

, we obtain 

O␀∆ 2 w∆Sw+1 

> n

 .

This proves the lemma. 

Lemma 13. Let Dn = {G1, . . . , G n} and let Sn = Pni=1 |V (Gi)|. Assume that every Gi has maximum degree at most ∆. For fixed w and ∆, the number of predicate symbols introduced by Algorithm 3 at stage n is bounded by a polynomial in Sn.

32 Proof. Algorithm 3 introduces one predicate symbol pρ for each ρ ∈ F ∪{ ρ∅}.

At stage n, the set F is either unchanged from an earlier stage or is set to BRep w(Dj ) for some j ≤ n. Since Dj ⊆ Dn, we have BRep w(Dj ) ⊆

BRep w(Dn). Hence |F| ≤ | BRep w(Dn)|. Therefore the number of predicate symbols introduced is at most |BRep w(Dn)|+1 . By Lemma 12, this quantity is bounded by a polynomial in Sn.

Lemma 14. Let Dn = {G1, . . . , G n} and let Sn = Pni=1 |V (Gi)|. As-sume that every Gi has maximum degree at most ∆. For fixed s, t, w, d, ∆,the number of bounded fixed-interface clause candidates considered by Algo-rithm 3 at stage n is bounded by a polynomial in Sn. This count includes the zero-body case ℓ = 0 .Proof. Let Nn = |BRep w(Dn)|. By Lemma 12, Nn is bounded by a polyno-mial in Sn.At stage n, the current predicate basis F is either unchanged from an earlier stage or is equal to BRep w(Dj ) for some j ≤ n. Hence |F| ≤ Nn. Thus the number of available predicate representatives is at most |F| +1 ≤ Nn +1 ,

where the additional one is ρ∅.A clause candidate considered by Algorithm 3 has the form pρ(H) ←

pρ1 (H1), . . . , p ρℓ (Hℓ), where 0 ≤ ℓ ≤ t, and all predicate representatives are chosen from F ∪ { ρ∅}. For a fixed ℓ, the number of choices of the head and body predicate representatives is at most ( Nn + 1) ℓ+1 ≤ (Nn + 1) t+1 .

For fixed parameters s, t, w, d and finite alphabets Σ V , ΣE , the number of syntactic fixed-interface graph-pattern shapes H, H 1, . . . , H ℓ satisfying the bounds s, w, d is bounded by a constant depending only on these fixed parameters. The correspondence between the variable hyperedges of H and the body star patterns is also bounded by a constant depending only on 

s and t. Moreover, the possible equality patterns among variable labels occurring on the boundedly many variable hyperedges are bounded by a constant depending only on s.Therefore the total number of bounded fixed-interface clause candidates, including the case ℓ = 0, is bounded by c Ptℓ=0 (Nn +1) ℓ+1 for some constant 

c depending only on s, t, w, d, ΣV , ΣE . This is bounded by a polynomial in 

Nn, and hence by a polynomial in Sn.

Lemma 15. Let Dn = {G1, . . . , G n} and let Sn = Pni=1 |V (Gi)|. Assume that every Gi has maximum degree at most ∆. For fixed s, t, w, d, ∆, the total number of membership queries used by Algorithm 3 at stage n is bounded by a polynomial in Sn.

33 Proof. Let Nn = |BRep w(Dn)|. By Lemma 12, Nn is bounded by a polyno-mial in Sn.At stage n, the current predicate basis F is either unchanged from an earlier stage or is equal to BRep w(Dj ) for some j ≤ n. Hence |F| ≤ Nn.

Moreover, Algorithm 4 sets R = BRep w(Dn) at stage n, and therefore 

|R| = Nn.

First, consider the construction of the observation table Obs F,R. For each pair ( ρ, σ ) ∈ F × R , Algorithm 2 issues at most one membership query, namely for can( ρ) ⊙ can( σ) ∈ L∗, provided that the composition is defined. Hence the number of membership queries used for the observation table is at most |F| |R| ≤ N 2

> n

.

Next, consider the clause-admission tests in Algorithm 3. By Lemma 14, the number of bounded fixed-interface clause candidates considered at stage 

n, including the zero-body case ℓ = 0, is bounded by a polynomial in Sn.For a zero-body candidate pρ(H) ←, Algorithm 3 issues at most one membership query, namely for can( ρ) ⊙ H ∈ L∗, provided that the composi-tion is defined. Therefore the total number of membership queries used for zero-body candidates is bounded by the number of clause candidates, and hence by a polynomial in Sn.Now consider a non-fact candidate C = pρ(H) ← pρ1 (H1), . . . , p ρℓ (Hℓ),

where 1 ≤ ℓ ≤ t. For each i = 1 , . . . , ℓ , let xi be the unique variable label occurring in the star graph pattern Hi, and put X(C) = {xi | 1 ≤ i ≤

ℓ}. Algorithm 3 checks families ( σx)x∈X(C) ∈ R X(C). Since |R| = Nn, the number of such families is N |X(C)| 

> n

. Since |X(C)| ≤ ℓ ≤ t, this number is at most N tn.

For each such family, Algorithm 3 issues at most one head membership query, namely for can( ρ) ⊙ Real( H, {x := can( σx)}x∈X(C)) ∈ L∗, whenever the body observations are positive and the realization is defined. Hence the number of membership queries used for a single non-fact candidate is at most N tn.

Multiplying this bound by the polynomially many clause candidates from Lemma 14, we obtain a polynomial bound in Sn for all non-fact clause-admission queries. Combining the observation-table queries, the zero-body clause queries, and the non-fact clause-admission queries, the total number of membership queries used by Algorithm 3 at stage n is bounded by a polynomial in Sn.

Lemma 16. At each stage n, the total time required to construct the hy-pothesis (Γ n, p ρ∅ ) from G1, . . . , G n is bounded by a polynomial in Sn =Pni=1 |V (Gi)|.

34 Proof. We estimate the total time required by Algorithm 4 to construct the hypothesis (Γ n, p ρ∅ ) from G1, . . . , G n.

First consider the update test in Algorithm 4: Dn ⊈ L(bΓ, p ρ∅ ), where bΓ = ConstructGamma( F, R). This test is carried out by checking, for each graph 

Gi ∈ Dn, whether Gi ∈ L(bΓ, p ρ∅ ). For each such graph, the membership problem for ( bΓ, p ρ∅ ) is solvable in polynomial time by Proposition 1 and Corollary 2, since the structural parameters are fixed. Since |Dn| = n ≤ Sn,

the total cost of the update test is bounded by a polynomial in Sn.Next, if the update test succeeds, Algorithm 4 constructs BRep w(Dn)and assigns it to F. Regardless of whether F is updated, the algorithm also constructs R = BRep w(Dn) at stage n. By Lemma 12, BRep w(Dn) can be constructed in time polynomial in Sn. Moreover, its size is also bounded by a polynomial in Sn.It remains to estimate the cost of constructing Γ n = ConstructGamma( F, R).

By Lemma 13, the number of predicate symbols introduced by Algorithm 3 is bounded by a polynomial in Sn. By Lemma 14, the number of bounded fixed-interface clause candidates considered by Algorithm 3, including the zero-body case ℓ = 0, is bounded by a polynomial in Sn.The construction of the observation table and the clause-admission tests use membership queries to L∗. By Lemma 15, the total number of member-ship queries used by Algorithm 3 at stage n is bounded by a polynomial in 

Sn.For each such membership query, the queried graph is either of the form can( ρ) ⊙ can( σ) for the observation table, or of the form can( ρ) ⊙ H

for a zero-body clause candidate, or of the form can( ρ) ⊙ Real( H, {x := can( σx)}x∈X(C)) for a non-fact clause candidate C. Since the structural parameters are fixed and all representatives used at stage n come from BRep w(Dn), the size of each queried graph is bounded by a polynomial in Sn. Hence, by Proposition 1, Corollary 2, and Luks’ polynomial-time isomorphism algorithm for bounded-valence graphs [5], each membership query can be processed in polynomial time. All remaining operations in Algorithm 3, such as checking whether com-positions and realizations are defined and constructing the corresponding graphs with interface, are performed over polynomially many candidates and graphs of polynomial size. Hence their total cost is also polynomial in 

Sn.Combining the above bounds, every component of the construction of (Γ n, p ρ∅ ) at stage n is bounded by a polynomial in Sn. Therefore the total time required to compute (Γ n, p ρ∅ ) at stage n is bounded by a polynomial 35 in Sn.

Theorem 2 (Polynomial-time update) . Algorithm 4 has polynomial-time update in the sense of Definition 21. Proof. Let L∗ ∈ FICSL FCP ∆ (m, s, t, w, d ), and let G1, G 2, . . . be a positive presentation of L∗.For each stage n, Lemma 16 shows that the total time required to con-struct the n-th hypothesis (Γ n, p ρ∅ ) from the initial segment G1, . . . , G n is bounded by a polynomial in Sn = Pni=1 |V (Gi)|. Therefore there exists a polynomial P such that, for every n ≥ 1, the time required by Algorithm 4 to output (Γ n, p ρ∅ ) after reading G1, . . . , G n is at most P (Sn). Hence Algo-rithm 4 has polynomial-time update in the sense of Definition 21. The preceding bounds show that the learner stores only polynomially many ordered boundary representations in BRep w(Dn), generates only poly-nomially many predicate symbols and bounded fixed-interface clause can-didates, and performs only polynomially many membership queries at each stage. Combined with the parameterized polynomial bound for the mem-bership problem, this yields polynomial-time update for Algorithm 4. 

# 5 Conclusions 

We have studied the learnability of graph languages generated by fixed-interface clause systems from the viewpoint of distributional learning. This paper is a full journal version of the ILP 2016 conference paper [7], in which all proofs were omitted. It extends that work in four respects: complete proofs are provided, the framework is reformulated using fixed-interface clause systems and ordered boundary representations that make the bound-ary structure explicit, the bounded treewidth assumption of [7] is shown to be unnecessary for the present learning argument, and an explicit param-eter tuple (∆ , m, s, t, w, d ) is introduced to trace the structural restrictions responsible for learnability and polynomial-time update. A key point of the present approach is that, although the target language consists of plain graphs, the learner operates on graphs with interfaces ob-tained by cutting observed graphs along designated boundary vertices. This explicit boundary structure is what makes it possible to define context can-didates in a graph setting and to carry out a parameterized complexity analysis of the update process. In this sense, the present formulation makes explicit a structural ingredient that is only implicit in fragment-based graph learning formalisms. 36 Within this framework, we considered a bounded class of graph lan-guages satisfying the finite context property under a bounded-degree as-sumption. The bounds were made explicit through the parameter tuple (∆ , m, s, t, w, d ), which controls both the shape of the target clause systems and the size of the candidate hypothesis space. We presented an oracle-guided learning algorithm based on ordered boundary representations in-duced by the observed positive examples and on membership-query-based clause admission. The correctness proof was organized into separate steps: target contexts eventually appear in the observed sample, target clauses are reconstructed over the corresponding predicate representatives, and spurious non-fact clauses are eventually excluded by membership queries. From these ingredients, we obtained identification in the limit from positive data and membership queries, together with polynomial-time update, for the class 

FICSL FCP ∆ (m, s, t, w, d ). Several directions remain open. First, the present analysis is restricted to unary predicate symbols. It remains to investigate whether analogous parameterized bounds can be obtained for clause systems with higher-arity predicates. Second, it would be desirable to make more explicit the formal correspondence between fixed-interface clause systems and regular formal graph systems, and to clarify in a precise form how the current formulation preserves the language generation mechanism of the regular FGS setting. Third, the degree-safe condition introduced in this paper is a sufficient but not necessary condition for bounded degree. Identifying weaker conditions that still guarantee bounded degree under recursive derivations is an impor-tant direction. Fourth, and most intriguingly, the present work suggests a connection to factor-critical graphs. A factor-critical graph cannot be gen-erated by a fixed-interface clause system in general, since such graphs are not context-free in the graph-theoretic sense. However, certain restricted subclasses of factor-critical graphs, whose ear decomposition structure ad-mits a fixed interface assignment, may be expressible within the present framework. Identifying precisely which subclasses of factor-critical graphs fall within FICSL ∆(m, s, t, w, d ), and determining the boundary of what fixed-interface clause systems can express, is a challenging and promising direction for future work. 

# Acknowledgments
